flashcards.back: A program that manages computer hardware, provides a basis for application programs, and acts as an intermediary between the computer user and the computer hardware. It controls and coordinates the use of hardware among various application programs and users.

flashcards.front: What are the four components of a computer system?
flashcards.back:
1. Hardware (CPU, memory, I/O devices) - provides basic computing resources
2. Operating System - controls and coordinates hardware use
3. Application Programs (compilers, browsers, games) - define how resources solve user problems
4. Users (people, machines, other computers)

flashcards.front: What does the OS do from the User View perspective?
flashcards.back: From the user view, the OS is designed for ease of use and good performance. For a PC, the OS maximizes the work the user can perform. For shared systems (mainframes), it maximizes resource utilization. For embedded systems, it runs without direct user interaction.

flashcards.front: What does the OS do from the System View perspective?
flashcards.back: From the system view, the OS is a resource allocator that manages CPU time, memory space, file storage, and I/O devices. It decides between conflicting requests for efficient and fair resource use. It is also a control program that manages execution of user programs to prevent errors and improper use.
The kernel is the one program running at all times on the computer. It is the core of the operating system. Everything else is either a system program (ships with the OS) or an application program.

flashcards.front: How does computer system organization handle I/O?
flashcards.back: CPU and device controllers connect through a common bus to shared memory. Each controller manages a specific device. The device controller has a local buffer. CPU moves data between main memory and local buffers. The device controller informs CPU that it has finished via an interrupt.

flashcards.front: What is the interrupt mechanism in computer system organization?
flashcards.back: Interrupts transfer control to the interrupt service routine through the interrupt vector (a table of handler addresses). The CPU state (registers, program counter) is saved before handling the interrupt and restored after. A trap is a software-generated interrupt caused by an error or system call.

DMA is used for high-speed I/O devices. The device controller transfers blocks of data directly between its buffer and main memory without CPU intervention. Only one interrupt is generated per block rather than one per byte, freeing the CPU for other work.

flashcards.front: What is the storage hierarchy in a computer system?
flashcards.back:
Organized by speed, cost, and volatility (fastest to slowest):
Registers > Cache > Main Memory > Solid-State Disk > Magnetic Disk > Optical Disk > Magnetic Tape
Caching copies frequently used data to faster storage. Main memory is volatile; secondary storage is non-volatile.

Multiprogramming organizes jobs so the CPU always has one to execute. Jobs are kept in a job pool on disk; the OS picks and runs one. When it waits for I/O, the OS switches to another job.
Time-sharing (multitasking) extends this so the CPU switches between jobs so frequently that users can interact with each job while it is running.
